Key Takeaways:
Screencap video vs. full motion video
• Screen capture is often easier than full motion for many reasons - lots of logistics
and technical pieces with full motion
• It can be a lot easier to deliver a script with a screen cap video
• If this is your first launch or you're just starting out you may want to script your
video tightly
• If you're going to deliver from a script with a screen capture you could read it off
your computer screen
• With screencap you can record the audio first, and then you do the on screen video
recording after
• A lot of people dislike being on camera and have many worries – just practice
getting in front of the camera
• If that’s the case and you can’t get around it - screencap is the way to go
• It's generally easier for the viewer to connect with you if it’s full motion video
• It's harder to connect with someone if it's a screen capture video
• If you had never seen me in full motion, you just wouldn't have as close a
connection with me as you do
• If you need to edit something into a full motion video, you can actually record it as
a screencap and put a little section in the middle of your full motion video
• Screen capture can be a lot faster to create
• But if you want to use fancy animations it does get slower and it's a lot more effort
to create
• Important: you can mix full motion with screen capture video - I’ve done this a lot
for my launches
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
Okay, this is part two of Creating Prelaunch Content.
I want to talk about screen cap video vs full motion video.
This will be a big decision for a lot of people. Screen capture is a video that
captures your computer screen while you talk over it. It is often easier than full motion
for lots of reasons. For this video, I created a Keynote or a PowerPoint presentation, and
then I turned on my recorder and I hooked up my microphone, and I started talking.
If this was a full motion video, we'd have to put together some type of a video
shot/set.
It might be the shot where I'm at my whiteboard, but we have to put together a
shot. I have to get my camera. We have to get the lights all hooked up. I'll have to care
about how I look and have to get dressed in something nice.
There's just a lot more going on generally with a full motion, whereas a quick and
dirty screen cap can be a lot easier.
It can be a lot easier to deliver a script with a screen cap video.
This video is not scripted. We're going over material I know very, very well, so I
can hit the record button and start talking, and just deliver the content.
If this was Prelaunch Content, I want it to be content that is a lot more tightly
scripted. Even if it's just bullet points, I still have a pretty darn good idea of exactly how I
want to go through it.
If this is your first launch or you're just starting out, you probably want to script it
pretty darn tightly. Even now with all the experience I have, my Prelaunch videos are
pretty darn scripted. They're not word-for-word, especially in the teaching portions, I go
from bullet points, but usually the beginning of the video and the end of the video are
pretty darn scripted. Again, I'm usually not delivering line by line to the script, but I've at
least written it out and I've studied it quite a bit, and I'm ready to go with the script.
If you're going to deliver from a script with a screen capture, it's a lot easier for
you to be reading off a printed script.
Here's the other trick, often with Prelaunch videos, record your script FIRST.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 5 --

4
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
You'll actually record the audio first when there is no video going on. Then you'll
do your screen capture to the audio recording. You're doing one piece at a time. That's
just a lot easier than standing in front of a camera and delivering a scripted message.
If you're going to do a heavy content piece, something like this where I'm just
speaking through my PowerPoint’s, and I'm in a teaching mode you can record
audio/video all at once. But if it is a piece of Prelaunch Content that's tightly scripted,
then a lot of times you will record the audio first. When you do this, it's a lot easier. You
can just read it of your computer screen. You can read it of a printout. You can't do that
as easily with a full motion video.
The thing about full motion video is some people really don't like to be on
camera.
They find that full motion is really painful. A lot of time people will say, "I'll start
shooting full motion when I lose 20 pounds," or something like that. A lot of people just
don't like how they look.
They're concerned about what to do with their hands. They're concerned about
what to do with their eyes. They're concerned about the way they dress. The reality is you
for most of that stuff, you shouldn't worry about it that much, and just start getting in
front of the camera, and practicing. Pretty soon, it will become natural, but a lot of times,
people don't like to be in camera. If that's the case, then screen capture is the way to go.
Now, in favor of full motion video it's generally easier for the viewer to connect
with you. Just like right now, you can't see me. You can't see the expression on my face.
You can't see the gestures I'm making. It's harder to connect with someone if it's a screen
capture video.
Now, since you've seen a lot of me in full motion video, and you've seen me both
in my Prelaunch, and we'll use a lot of full motion videos within this course. You've seen
me, so you've probably built up that connection, but if you had never seen me in full
motion, you wouldn't have as close a connection with me as you do with full motion.
Generally, it's a lot easier to edit screen capture after the fact. If I forget to say
something here, I can literally go back and just record another audio piece, and insert it
right into this video, and you would never know the difference.
Just by the nature of this, the editing process of screen capture, is a lot easier to
do. If you shoot a full motion video, then all of a sudden like a day or two from now you
realized, "Oh I forgot about that one piece." It's harder to go and put that and edit that
piece in later after the fact. It's possible, but you have to worry about being perfectly the
same, your clothes being perfectly the same, your hair being perfectly the same. It's just a
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 5 --

5
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
lot harder to do something like that after the fact.
In fact, if you find the situation where you do need to edit something into a full
motion video, a lot of times the best way to do it is to actually record it as a screen cap
and put a little screen cap section in the middle of your full motion video. Now, it can be
awkward if you have a 15-minute video, and then you just have one piece of screen cap in
the middle, so you have to play with that. You might have to add a several different
screen caps segments, but you could do that.
The overall message is that it is easier to edit screen capture after the fact than it
is to do the same with full motion video.
Screen capture can be a lot faster to create. With this video I just turned on a
recorder and I'm speaking into it. If it is a tightly scripted piece of Prelaunch Content, it
can still be a lot easier, a lot faster to create than a full motion video. Now, on the other
hand, screen capture can be slower to create. If you want to use a whole bunch of fancy
animations, if you want to have different pictures flying in and out, you can get very, very
fancy with screen capture video. Once you start to do that it does get slower and it's a lot
more effort to create.
Another thing that is important to remember is you can mix full motion with
screen capture video. I have done this a lot. Probably the last six or eight launches, I've
done something like this. For me, I often do this blueprint video at the end of my third
piece of Prelaunch Content traditionally, and I'll have a full motion intro where I'm
talking directly to the camera about what the viewers are going to get, and then we'll cut
into a screen capture video, and I'll deliver a whole bunch of content via screen capture.
Then often at the end, I'll come back and do a full motion conclusion.
There's all different ways to do this. You can do something that's almost all full
motion with some screen cap pieces that come in if you're talking about a certain bullet
points. You can get very creative. You can mix and match the two of them.
